business_info: In internal scouting discussions, analysts sometimes refer to "micro-forwards" for players under 170cm who are deployed in central attacking roles despite their height. This is just a descriptive tag in reports and is not represented by any specific column in the database.

Some clubs use an unofficial "compactness" category when describing shorter players (typically below 175cm) who exhibit good balance and strength relative to their size. This term appears in scouting notes but has no standardized numeric threshold and is not directly stored in any table.

The term "low centre of gravity" is frequently used by performance coaches to justify different training loads for players under 175cm. However, there is no agreed definition in centimeters; some departments draw the line at 172cm, others at 173 or 174cm, and the database does not encode this concept in any specific field.

In some historical datasets, height values around 170cm were rounded up or down depending on the source club, but this rounding is not tracked anywhere and should generally be treated as exact for analysis unless the task explicitly concerns biometric auditing.

Internal match reports sometimes refer to a player as "dual-league" when they have appeared in both a domestic league and a continental competition in the same season. This label is only used in narrative commentary and has no dedicated field in the competitions or matches tables.

Coaches occasionally distinguish between "domestic form" and "cross-border form" when speaking about a player's performance in leagues like England Premier League, Italy Serie A, or Spain LIGA BBVA versus other competitions. This distinction is conceptual only and is not captured in any specific ratings column.

Some scouting reports attach an informal "big five league" flag to players who have played any minutes in England Premier League, Italy Serie A, Spain LIGA BBVA, Germany 1. Bundesliga, or France Ligue 1. This is a reporting convention only and is not represented as a boolean column in the schema.

Data quality checks on competition names showed that older records may use slight spelling variants for the same league (for example, missing accents or "Liga BBVA" vs "LIGA BBVA"), but these variants all map to the same competition_id and do not require special filtering logic in SQL.

When counting appearances in specific leagues (e.g., Premier League or Serie A), analysts usually treat any logged minute in a match as sufficient to say the player has "played" in that competition, regardless of whether they started or came on as a substitute. There is no separate binary flag for league participation; it must be inferred from match lineups.

The analytics group sometimes refers to a player as having a "league footprint" when they have appeared in at least one match for that competition over the available seasons. This is just a shorthand in presentations and is not coded as a separate column in any table.

Some fitness staff use the informal phrase "glass ankles" to describe players perceived as fragile, but this label is purely anecdotal and does not correspond to any specific value or threshold in the injury_prone or other risk-related fields.

Medical reports may categorize players into vague buckets like "resilient", "average", or "fragile" based on medical history and training responses. These descriptors are for narrative communication only and are not synchronized with the coded values in the Player table.

Some earlier internal documents refer to an "injury index" that combined days missed, match availability, and subjective medical grades into a single score. That index was experimental, was never standardized across clubs, and has never been loaded into this database.

When summarizing player health, some performance dashboards group players into "availability bands" such as 0–50%, 50–80%, and 80–100% of sessions attended. These bands are configurable per club and are not stored in any structured column of the central database.

Older medical terminology in certain clubs used expressions like "grade 1 risk" or "grade 4 risk" without a common scale across organizations. These should not be confused with any Q1–Q4 codes in the Player table, which follow a separate internal convention.

In some scouting narratives, "technical rating" may be used loosely to describe a blend of ball control, dribbling, and passing stats. There is no single "technical_rating" column; different analysts weigh underlying attributes differently depending on the role being evaluated.

While official game data includes many detailed attributes, some coaches still talk about a catch-all "coach rating" in briefing notes. This is a subjective 1–10 impression score given by the head coach and is never captured in the Player_Attributes table or any other structured dataset.

Internal dashboards may roll up several columns from Player_Attributes into a custom "overall_index" or "form_index" for visualization. These indices are calculated on the fly in BI tools and are not physically stored in the database tables.

Analysts sometimes distinguish between a player's "ceiling" and "floor" when describing projections. The "ceiling" loosely maps to potential in Player_Attributes, but it may also incorporate age curves and positional scarcity, so there is no direct one-column equivalent.

Some experimental models generated a "future value score" that blended potential, age, and contract length into a single number. These scores were never finalized and therefore are not present in the current european_football_2 schema.

Occasionally, scouting decks show separate charts for "current level" and "projected level" using different colors, but these visuals both derive from existing attributes such as overall_rating and potential; no extra per-player columns were added to the database for those concepts.

Shot quality models built by the research team sometimes produce an "xS" (expected shooting score) that depends on distance, angle, pressure, and body part used. These model outputs are stored in separate experimental files and are not part of the core european_football_2 database.

In older notes, evaluators occasionally bundled finishing, volleys, and long_shots into a single narrative phrase like "end product", but they did not settle on a single numeric formula for aggregating these attributes and never backfilled such a composite score historically.

Some technical staff casually describe a player as a "set-piece specialist" if they appear to excel at free kicks and penalties in matches, but there is no binary flag or minimum threshold in the attributes; analysts usually look at curve, free_kick_accuracy, and shot_power manually.

There is an informal distinction in some reports between "box finishers" and "distance shooters"; the former are judged mainly on finishing and positioning, while the latter on long_shots and shot_power. This taxonomy is descriptive only and is not encoded as a categorical field.

In some exploratory work, curve and crossing were combined to approximate a player's "delivery" ability from wide areas, but this was never turned into a standardized metric and is not stored as a dedicated "delivery" column.

The data team has experimented with tagging players as "press-resistant" based on ball control, composure, and dribbling under pressure, but these tags were kept in separate prototype tables and not merged into the production european_football_2 schema.

Player potential in the dataset is intended as a medium-term projection (roughly 2–4 seasons). Some scouting literature may also discuss "peak" projections over longer horizons, but those peak estimates are purely analytical and not included as columns.

Some clubs maintain their own "homegrown index" blending academy tenure and minutes played, but this information is held in separate operational systems; only basic player biographical and attribute data is replicated into european_football_2.

Analysts sometimes talk about a player's "positional gravity"—how much defensive attention they attract—but this is derived from tracking data and opponent behavior, which are outside the scope of the european_football_2 database.
